coredata.dc:title: Improved charge transport through 2D framework in fully condensed carbon nitride for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Hui
Cao, Yuqi
Li, Zonglin
Gao, Yang
Shangguan, Li
Sun, Jianhua
Lang, Leiming
Lei, Weiwei
coredata.dc:description: The 1D molecular structure of traditional graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) leads to the space-confined transport of charge carriers along the chains and prohibits transport across the chains. Therefore, the formation of 2D network which can provide more convenient transfer paths is of great desirable. In this work, a fully condensed carbon nitride with the real 2D framework was successfully developed by post-calcining 1D GCN in a LiCl/KCl molten salt. The newly formed 2D molecular structure significantly promoted the charge transfer and separation, resulting in remarkably enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. Moreover, N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) incorporation can be simultaneously realized via the simple one step “co-dissolution and recrystallization” method. Especially, the as-prepared 2D-CN/NGQDs sample presents a further increased photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate up to about 30 times that of pristine GCN. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for H2 evolution of 2D-CN/NGQDs reaches 36 % at 420 nm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nitride
Charge transport
2D framework
Photocatalysis
Hydrogen production

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702134 and 21373103 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20170310 ), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. 21KJA610003 ), and the Australian Research Council Discovery Program ( DP190103290 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-c) Illustration of the synthetic procedure of 2D-CN from GCN and (d) the photographs of the two samples treated with concentrated sulfuric acid.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) FT-IR spectra, XPS high-resolution (c) C 1 s, and (d) N 1 s spectra of GCN and 2D-CN.
(a) steady-state and (b) time-resolved transient PL spectra of GCN, 2D-CN, and 2D-CN/NGQDs.
(a) SEM and (b, c) TEM images of 2D-CN. (d) SEM image, (e) elemental mappings, and (f-h) TEM images of 2D-CN/NGQDs. (i) Enlarged area marked by white dashed box in (h).
(a) Transient photocurrent responses, (b) EIS Nyquist plots, (c) UV–vis DRS and (d) band structure alignments of GCN, 2D-CN, and 2D-CN/NGQDs.

(a) Photocatalytic H2 evolution rates of different samples under visible light (λ greater than 420 nm) irradiation. (b) Photocatalytic stability test and (c) wavelength-dependent AQE of 2D-CN/NGQDs. (d) Direct comparison of AQE at 420 nm between this work and previous studies.

head.ce:title: Improved charge transport through 2D framework in fully condensed carbon nitride for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Institute of Advanced Functional Materials for Energy, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou 213001, Jiangsu Province, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering
Institute of Advanced Functional Materials for Energy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Environmental Engineering, Institute of Advanced Functional Materials for Energy, Jiangsu University of Technology, Changzhou 213001, Jiangsu Province, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials of Nanjing, Nanjing Xiaozhuang University, Nanjing, 211171, Jiangsu Province, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Laboratory of Advanced Functional Materials of Nanjing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria 3216, Australia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Frontier Materials, Deakin University, Waurn Ponds, Victoria 3216, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The 1D molecular structure of traditional graphitic carbon nitride (GCN) leads to the space-confined transport of charge carriers along the chains and prohibits transport across the chains. Therefore, the formation of 2D network which can provide more convenient transfer paths is of great desirable. In this work, a fully condensed carbon nitride with the real 2D framework was successfully developed by post-calcining 1D GCN in a LiCl/KCl molten salt. The newly formed 2D molecular structure significantly promoted the charge transfer and separation, resulting in remarkably enhanced photocatalytic hydrogen production activity. Moreover, N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) incorporation can be simultaneously realized via the simple one step “co-dissolution and recrystallization” method. Especially, the as-prepared 2D-CN/NGQDs sample presents a further increased photocatalytic hydrogen evolution rate up to about 30 times that of pristine GCN. The apparent quantum efficiency (AQE) for H2 evolution of 2D-CN/NGQDs reaches 36 % at 420 nm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Photocatalytic hydrogen production from water is regarded as an ideal pathway for converting unlimited solar energy into clean chemical energy [1,2]. In recent years, carbon nitride (CN) has emerged as a polymeric semiconductor photocatalyst with attractive photocatalytic hydrogen performance [3–5]. The intrinsic structures and properties of CN have been studied and gradually understood over the past years [6–10].

It is known that the traditional, thermal polycondensation of nitrogen-containing precursors usually produces a graphitic CN (GCN). This GCN is composed of one-dimensional (1D) chains of tri-s-triazine units linked by imide groups. The different chains are interconnected by hydrogen bonds to form a “pseudo” two-dimensional (2D) arrangement [11,12] as shown in Fig. 1 a. It is the incomplete polycondensation resulting in the low condensation degree (1D molecular structure) and the presence of abundant unreacted amino groups. The 1D molecular structure leads to the space-confined transport of charge carriers along the chains and prohibits transport across the chains [13]. The large numbers of amino groups act as recombination centers [14]. These two key aspects greatly decrease the photocatalytic activity of GCN.

Therefore, forming covalent bonds between different chains through deamination reaction to achieve a real 2D molecular structure would be an effective approach to improve the photoinduced charge separation and transfer. However, it is hard to obtain a fully condensed 2D structure by simply prolonging the calcination time. The major problem is that after the formation of the 1D chains, the mass transfer resistance becomes more serious, thus the deamination reaction is even more difficult.

It is worth mentioning that a fully condensed 2D polytriazine imide (PTI) has been prepared in a LiCl/KCl molten salt [15–17]. During the molten chloride assisted polymerization process, the deamination reaction is greatly accelerated. As a result, the s-triazine unites are linked by imide groups before they are transferred into tri-s-triazine blocks. Consequently, an s-triazine based 2D framework is achieved (The structure of PTI is shown in Fig. S1).

Motivated by the above, we suppose that the molten salt treatment would enable the further polycondensation of the 1D chains in GCN as shown in Fig. 1a-c. After dissolved in the molten chloride (LiCl/KCl, m.p. = 352 °C), the deamination reaction of GCN would be promoted, leading to the formation of new covalent bonds between different chains. Therefore, the highly condensed 2D-CN can be obtained.

Melamine (99.0 %), KCl (99.5 %), concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95.0–98.0 %), chloroplatinic acid (H2PtCl6·6H2O, 99.0 %), and triethanolamine (99.0 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. LiCl (99.0 %) was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., ltd. All the chemicals were used without further purification.

GCN was prepared by the thermal polycondensation of melamine: 5.0 g melamine was placed into a covered crucible, and then heated at 550 °C for 4 h with a rate of 2.2 °C·min−1 in a muffled furnace. After cooled to room temperature, the product was ground in a mortar and collected.

PTI was prepared by a slight modification of the procedure reported in the previous literature [18]: 1.0 g melamine were ground together with 10 g LiCl/KCl (45/55 wt%) and the resulting mixture was put into a crucible, heated at 550 °C for 4 h with a rate of 2.2 °C·min−1 in a muffle furnace. The obtained product was washed with abundant hot water several times, collected by filtration, and then dried at 60 °C.

The 2D-CN sample was prepared by a molten salt post-calcining method. Typically, 1.0 g GCN was thoroughly ground together with 10 g LiCl/KCl (45/55 wt%). The mixed powders were heated at 550 °C for 4 h with a rate of 2.2 °C·min−1 in a muffled furnace. The obtained product was washed with abundant hot water several times, collected by filtration, and then dried at 60 °C.
The synthesis of 2D-CN/NGQDs followed the above procedure, except that 5 mg N-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were added to 1.0 g GCN before grinding.

To reveal the correlation between the photocatalytic performance and the amounts of NGQDs in the material system, 2D-CN/NGQDs-x (x = 2.5, 5, 10, 20) were prepared following the above procedure except that x mg NGQDs was used as the precursor. Note The initial amount of NGQDs in 2D-CN/NGQDs is 5 mg, and the 2D-CN/NGQDs sample in the manuscript is 2D-CN/NGQDs-5.
NGQDs was prepared following the procedure in the previous literature [19].

As demonstrated in Fig. 1a-c, the 2D-CN sample is derived from GCN through the molten chloride introduced reconstruction strategy. The increased cross-linking degree of 2D-CN is supported by an acid dissolution experiment. As shown in Fig. 1d, the prepared GCN with 1D structure can be dissolved in concentrated sulfuric acid at 100 °C (The detailed procedure is described in the supporting information). This is due to the protonation of the amino groups leading to the breaking of hydrogen bonds between different chains. Thus, the dispersion of the separated 1D chains of GCN looks like a “solution”. In contrast, PTI is a compound with the known 2D framework [15,20,21], and concentrated sulfuric acid cannot break the covalent bonds in PTI. Therefore, PTI maintained its 2D structure and was not dissolved under the same condition (Fig. S1). For the 2D-CN sample, an obvious insolubility was observed, indicating its highly condensed 2D structure different from that of GCN.

The elemental analysis (EA) was performed to further evidence the increased linking degree of 2D-CN compared to that of GCN. Generally, three species of tri-s-triazine based carbon nitrides were developed according to the previous literature [22]: traditional GCN (1D melon-based carbon nitride), poly(heptazine imide) (PHI), and fully condensed CN. The basic unites of these carbon nitrides are similar. The main difference is the cross-linking degree, which can be distinguished by the different C/N atomic ratios. The theoretical C/N molar ratios of GCN, PHI, and fully condensed CN are 0.667, 0.706, and 0.750, respectively. As seen in Table S2, the C/N molar ratio of the GCN sample is 0.664, quite close to the theoretical value. The C/N molar ratio of 2D-CN is 0.720, which is between the theoretical values of PHI and fully condensed CN. Therefore, the 2D-CN sample isn’t the ideal fully condensed form. But it shows an increased cross-linking degree than that of 2D PHI.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the prepared GCN and 2D-CN are shown in Fig. 2 a. The GCN sample presents two distinct peaks at 13.0° and 27.5°, corresponding to its (100) and (002) planes, respectively [23]. For the 2D-CN sample, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of (002) peak is obviously narrower than that of GCN (Fig. S3), which is caused by the structural reconstruction with improved crystallinity [11,24]. In addition, this peak shifts slightly to 28.1°, reflecting a decreased interlayer distance [25,26] of 0.317 nm to that (0.324 nm) of GCN. This change is most likely owing to the increased interaction between the layers [27,28]. It is worth noting that the (100) peak shifts from 13.0° to 8.0°, indicating an obviously different in-plane network from that of GCN. This is in line with the fact that GCN displays an 1D structure while the sufficient condensation results in the 2D framework of 2D-CN.

The chemical structures of the samples were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. As shown in Fig. 2b, both GCN and 2D-CN exhibit similar characteristic peaks, indicating that the molten salt treatment maintains the core chemical structures of carbon nitride. However, compared to GCN, there are several obvious differences in the spectrum of 2D-CN. The weakened peaks at 3000–3500 cm−1 are due to the decreased amino/imide groups [29,30] in the newly formed 2D frameworks of 2D-CN. The new peaks at 2180 cm−1 correspond to the generated cyano groups, while the peaks at 1155 and 995 cm−1 correspond to the incorporation of metal ions [31–33].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectrum of GCN suggests the presence of C, N, and O elements, while the spectrum of 2D-CN shows the existence of C, N, O and K (Fig. S4). It reveals that the molten salt treatment introduced K+ into the prepared 2D-CN samples, in line with the FT-IR results. The doping of K+ in the CN frameworks can increase the electronic delocalization and further improve the mobility of charge carriers. In the C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2c), the GCN sample exhibits two peaks at 288.2 and 284.8 eV, matched with C atoms in the tri-s-triazine units and graphitic carbon, respectively [34]. The 2D-CN sample displays an extra peak at 286.5 eV, originating from cyano groups [35]. In the N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2d), GCN shows three peaks. The peaks at 401.1 and 398.7 eV can be attributed to the N atoms of NC3 and CNC in tri-s-triazine units, respectively. The other peak at 399.8 eV is derived from the N atoms in amino/imide groups [24,36]. It is worth noting that the peak at 399.8 eV disappears in the spectrum of 2D-CN, indicating the decreased amino and imide groups. This result together with the EA, XRD and FT-IR analysis further proves the formation of 2D frameworks via deamination reaction in 2D-CN.

As we discussed above, the construction of 2D framework and decrease of amino groups play an important role in promoting photoinduced charge separation and transfer. The charge separation behavior can be investigated by the photoluminescence (PL) spectra. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the 2D-CN sample displays sharply decreased emission intensity compared to that of pristine GCN, indicating much less radiative charge recombination [37,38]. It is probably due to the greatly reduced recombination centers (amino groups) in the structures of 2D-CN. The time-resolved PL decay spectra were present in Fig. 3b, and the corresponding fitting results are listed in Table S3. The 2D-CN sample displays an average carrier lifetime of 0.38 ns, which is substantially shortened by nearly an order of magnitude relative to that of pristine GCN (3.74 ns). This indicates the highly efficient and fast charge carrier transfer [39,40] in 2D-CN, which should be owing to the formation of the 2D framework. Compared to the 1D chains in GCN, this 2D structure can provide convenient transfer paths for photoinduced electrons thus greatly facilitating the charge transport.

To extend the structural reconstruction strategy from pure CN to CN based photocatalysts, we take NGQDs as an example to demonstrate that the 2D structure and other materials incorporation can be simultaneously realized for CN via the simple one step “co-dissolution and recrystallization” method. The as-prepared 2D-CN/NGQDs composite shows similar structure properties and charge separation/separation behaviors to those of 2D-CN, which are revealed by XRD, FT-IR, PL spectra, and time-resolved transient PL spectra (Fig. S5, S6, and 3).

The morphology properties of the prepared samples were investigated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 4 a-c, 2D-CN features branched morphology composed of nanorods, which is quite different from that of GCN (Fig. S7a, b). The 2D-CN/NGQDs sample presents a similar morphology to that of 2D-CN, with well dispersed C, N, and K atoms (Fig. 4d-g). The morphology changes result in larger surface areas for 2D-CN and 2D-CN/NGQDs compared to that of GCN. As calculated from the N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms in Fig. S8, the BET specific surface areas (S BET) were determined to be 11, 60, and 61 m2 g−1 for GCN, 2D-CN, and 2D-CN/NGQDs, respectively. The successful incorporation of NGQDs was confirmed by the high-resolution TEM images of 2D-CN/NGQDs. As seen in Fig. 4h and i, randomly dispersed NGQDs can be observed on the surface of 2D-CN/NGQDs. The lattice space of 0.20 nm matches well with that of the NGQDs precursor in Fig. S7c.

The transient photocurrent responses of a photocatalyst can be directly related to its charge carrier separation efficiency. As displayed in Fig. 5 a, the photocurrent density of 2D-CN is about three times that of GCN, suggesting an obviously enhanced photocarrier separation efficiency [41,42]. 2D-CN/NGQDs exhibits an even higher photocurrent density, which could be also caused by the stronger light-absorption ability as we discussed later. Moreover, the improved charge transport in 2D-CN and 2D-CN/NGQDs can be also confirmed by the decreased radius in the electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) in Fig. 5b.

The light-harvesting capacities of GCN, 2D-CN, and 2D-CN/NGQDs were studied by UV–vis diffuse reflection spectra (DRS). As shown in Fig. 5c, the GCN sample exhibits a typical semiconductor-like absorption. The intense absorption below 460 nm can be assigned to the π → π* transition. It is obvious that the 2D-CN shows a remarkably enhanced π → π* absorption compared to that of GCN. The increased light absorption ability of a conjugated polymer is usually associated with its structural rigidity [24,43]. This result is also in line with our proposition that the GCN is composed of 1D chains while the 2D-CN sample displays a 2D structure. More interestingly, the 2D-CN/NGQDs sample presents even stronger absorptions in both ultraviolet and visible regions. The higher absorption ability is beneficial for harvesting light and improving the photocatalytic performance.

Tauc plots in Fig. S9a-c show that the band gaps of GCN, 2D-CN, and 2D-CN/NGQDs are 2.76, 2.71, and 2.72 eV, respectively. The corresponding conduction band (CB) positions were determined by Mott–Schottky plots (Fig. S9d-f). Therefore, the band structures of the samples can be calculated and illustrated in Fig. 5d. Compared to pristine GCN, the 2D-CN and 2D-CN/NGQDs samples prepared by the post molten salt treatment present an obviously negative shift in the CB, which leads to a larger thermodynamic driving force in the photocatalytic reduction reaction.

The photocatalytic H2 evolution reactions were carried out in the presence of triethanolamine (TEOA) as the sacrificial reagent and Pt as the co-catalyst under visible light (λ greater than 420 nm) irradiation. As shown in Fig. 6 a, GCN exhibits a moderate photocatalytic H2 evolution activity, which is due to the space-confined charge transfer along the 1D chains and the abundant amino groups as recombination centers. After the molten chloride induced deamination reactions, the obtained 2D-CN sample has achieved a 2D framework with greatly reduced amino groups. Therefore, it shows a significantly enhanced H2 evolution rate up to 119 μmol·h−1, which is 24.3 times that (4.9 μmol·h−1) of GCN. When small amount of NGQDs was simultaneously introduced into the system through the “co-dissolution” method in molten salt, the resulting 2D-CN/NGQDs presents a further improved photocatalytic activity, with the H2 evolution rate of 150 μmol·h−1, more than 30 times over the pristine GCN. Fig. 6b demonstrates that after four runs (12 h) of continuous visible light irradiation, no evident decay in activity was observed for 2D-CN/NGQDs.

In addition, to reveal the correlation between the photocatalytic performance and the amounts of NGQDs, 2D-CN/NGQDs-x (x = 2.5, 5, 10, 20) samples with different NGQDs contents were prepared. The XRD patterns and UV–vis DRS of the as-synthesized samples are shown in Fig. S10 and S11. It is obvious that the incorporation of suitable amount of NGQDs can improve the light harvesting ability of the system. The 2D-CN/NGQDs-5 sample with the highest optical absorption exhibits most efficient photocatalytic H2 production activity (Fig. S12. Note the 2D-CN/NGQDs sample in the manuscript is the optimal one, 2D-CN/NGQDs-5). Therefore, the improved photocatalytic activity of 2D-CN/NGQDs is mainly owing to the enhanced light absorption.

In Fig. 6c, the AQE values of 2D-CN/NGQDs match well with its UV–vis spectrum. This result conforms the fact that the detected hydrogen is indeed driven by the photogenerated electrons in the photocatalyst. In addition, the AQE of 2D-CN/NGQDs was determined to be 36 % at 420 nm, which is much higher than most of the reported tri-s-triazine based CN materials synthesized in molten salt (Fig. 6d) [24,29,32,33,44,45]. It is worth mentioning that although the super-high AQE of 60 % has been achieved in “sea water” (with additional 3 % NaCl), the AQE measured without NaCl is only 32 % at 420 nm [32], which is comparable with our result.

In summary, we have used 1D GCN as the precursor to prepare fully condensed carbon nitride via a molten salt post-calcining method. Two remarkable features were created in the obtained 2D-CN sample. One is the formation of 2D framework providing convenient transfer paths for photoinduced electrons. The other is the obviously reduced amino groups contributing to improved charge separation. The 2D-CN exhibits significantly enhanced H2 evolution rate, which is more than 20 times that of GCN. Such a great activity enhancement is due to the essentially different structures between 2D-CN and GCN, that is, the 2D and 1D molecular structures, respectively. Besides, simultaneous incorporation of NGQDs can be realized through this simple one step “co-dissolution and recrystallization” approach. The prepared 2D-CN/NGQDs shows even stronger light-absorption, better charge separation/transport, and more efficient photocatalytic H2 evolution activity. This study highlights the important role of the essential structure of CN, and provides a universal strategy for developing 2D CN based photocatalysts.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702134 and 21373103), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20170310), the Natural Science Foundation of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. 21KJA610003), and the Australian Research Council Discovery Program (DP190103290).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcat.2022.12.018.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
